questions.question: Your AI agent accidentally ran a destructive database query. Which architectural layer failed?
options: The model — it shouldn't have generated that query
The tools layer — the tool definition was too permissive (exposed write access when read-only would suffice)
The MCP layer — the protocol should block dangerous queries
The skills layer — the agent wasn't given the right instructions
questions.explanation: The tool set IS the permission model. Exposing a write-capable database tool when read-only suffices is like giving an intern root access. The fix is at the tools layer: scope the tool to read-only queries. The model will work within whatever boundaries you define.

questions.question: If you switch from Claude to a different AI model, your MCP server integrations need to be rebuilt from scratch.
questions.explanation: False. MCP is a standard protocol — any compliant client works with any compliant server. Your MCP servers survive a model switch. This is the vendor independence value of standardizing on MCP for AI integrations.

questions.question: From a governance perspective, what makes the tools architecture fundamentally different from giving an AI model direct API access?
options: Tools are faster than direct API calls
The model proposes actions but the host application decides whether to execute them — creating an auditable control point
The model can only use one tool at a time
questions.explanation: The model never executes anything directly. It emits a structured request, and the host application gates execution. This creates a natural audit point — every proposed action is logged, can be reviewed, and can be blocked. This is the fundamental trust model.
